column121: Gate agents used block seats [on this flight] for passenger. I was unable to sit in my assigned jumpseat due to gate agents selling seats.  We notified company and a Supervisor came down and talked to agents. The Supervisor then asked me; in front of the passengers; if I wanted to have a passenger taken off [the flight]. I was given the choices of Dead Head on the flight or sit in the jumpseat. I felt pressured to take a jumpseat; sharing with another Flight Attendant.A week later; I was notified that a crewmember working that flight had tested positive for COVID-19. I'm very upset that I was put in this position and put myself at risk. I still don't know if the Flight Attendant I sat next to on the jumpseat is the one who tested positive.

column121: Day 5; while enroute to the ZZZ layover hotel I received a message from a Captain I had dinner with on Day 0. I'll put this message in context by mentioning my First Officer and I met this Captain and his First Officer for dinner that evening.The message stated on Day 2; this particular Captain was notified by the company that the First Officer he flew with had tested positive for COVID. The First Officer had sent a message to this Captain confirming such. The ZZZ1 Chief Pilot was subsequently notified and a request was made for that office to contact my crew of this matter (I never received any communication about this event). This Captain was tested on Day 2; became symptomatic on Day 3 and obtained a positive test result on Day 5 prompting the message.Upon reviewing the message; I contacted my First Officer and the company On-duty Manager to notify each of the situation. Per company and CDC COVID protocol both the First Officer and I have been quarantined for a 14-day period commencing Day 0. As I compose this report; I am quarantined at the hotel; unable to travel home.The intent of this report is to question why didn't the company notify my crew of the COVID encounter? Apparently protocol wasn't followed or if it was; it should be modified to address this particular or similar scenarios. Flight crews should not be placed in a situation where they must quarantine away from home while on company business; especially if the company was previously notified to contact a crew which was exposed to a COVID positive employee. Had I not been received said message I would still be flying the line with the potential to infect others with this virus.
column125: Air carrier First Officer reported the crew was not notified by the company of exposure to a COVID-19 positive employee.

column119: Company Policy; Equipment / Tooling; Human Factors
column121: I was forced to deliver [aircraft type] differences class using Distance Learning to mechanics in ZZZ & ZZZ1 while I was in ZZZ2. X mechanics in ZZZ sat in a common area together with nonstudents. 5 mechanics in ZZZ1 sat in a common area together with nonstudents. The mechanics in ZZZ each had their own laptop; camera; microphone & speakers. The mechanics in ZZZ1 had one TV screen; one camera; one microphone and one speaker. Throughout class all the mechanics had to be muted due to poor audio quality; this made it impossible to tell if non class related conversations were occurring. Throughout class the mechanics in ZZZ cameras were not always displaying their face; making it impossible for me to observe their comprehension. Throughout class the mechanics in ZZZ1 were too far away from their shared camera; and often not visible on camera; making it impossible for me to observe their comprehension. Throughout class there was a lengthy delay in the audio to video; making class interaction difficult to the point it was nonexistent. During the test there was talking amongst students that the proctor was not controlling; and most students failed the test. The students were given a retest by the proctor without receiving remedial training. Based on these events I was not able to follow company policy and not able to accomplish the objectives on [the training].During the class the internet connection was not reliable enough to promote class participation. After class was over I received an email; and later a phone call from one of the AMTs that attended class. He had some questions regarding one of the systems on the [aircraft type] that were discussed during class. I explained the system in depth; to a level that goes above and beyond [the training]; but I am concerned the other mechanics may also have questions and whether they fully comprehended the objectives of [the training].The company has a desire to have a large number of mechanics trained on the [aircraft] and to save money; the company is forcing me to teach utilizing Distance Learning.Stop teaching the [aircraft type] via Distance Learning and return to in person classes.

column121: After flight I was informed that I had been exposed to COVID-19 the day prior to my 2 day trip. I called the flight operations manager and informed him of my exposure.  I made sure that he knew to contact the Captain on my trip as I know that he was going to his relative's home after work and that older people are more likely to have complications.  Additionally; I knew that he had a trip leaving the next day.  I was called by the on call assistant chief pilot; and told that the Captain would be contacted.  I failed to mention that I had dinner with another airline crew on my layover; as I had been concentrating on getting the Captain informed quickly.  Had either the flight operations manager or the assistant flight manager on call asked me about other interactions I would have listed the close contact I had with 2 other employees and been able to have them not show up to work for subsequent trips potentially exposing more crew members.  Additionally; I found out that the Captain was not informed about his exposure until after his return from his next trip.  I was surprised that given my reported exposure he was permitted to operate his subsequent trip.

column121: ATC on CPDLC (Controller Pilot Datalink Communications) gave us a climb and maintain FL 360 without an ATC controller assigning us a clearance via radio. column121: I was scheduled for the re-qualification X course. I requested a warm up sim and was denied by Person X going through my chief pilot office. Person X claims that warm up sims are a waste of time and don't improve an already high pass rate. Person X said training is designed to catch any lack of proficiency prior to the CQLOE [Continuing Qualification Line Operational Evaluation]; so a warm up sim isn't necessary as normal training will catch any lack of proficiency.I did the traditional re-qualification X course in month after being off the type X aircraft for X years. I only flew about 90 hours on the type X aircraft prior to the reduction of flying. I haven't flown since the middle of month. I thought requesting a warm up sim was reasonable considering my lack of recency/proficiency on the type X aircraft; and a new very short re-qualification course.The new re-qualification X course has several areas that aren't covered prior to the LOE [Line Operational Evaluation]. In the curriculum; it shows the initial 3 sims all start engines running on the runway. There is no practicing flows; FMS work on the ground; starting engines; or normal ground ops getting to the runway which are some of the most high risk areas. There is no time built into the curriculum to work on procedures for people that have [not] flown for 8-9 months and didn't have much recency.I feel the company is focused on pushing through as many people through as quickly as possible when the company let them lose currency. I don't feel that the new re-qualification X course is appropriate or safe for all pilots that can be trained under it. There should be other recency requirements for pilots to be trained under the new re-qualification X.

column113: Deviation / Discrepancy - Procedural FAR; Deviation / Discrepancy - Procedural Published Material / Policy
column121: During classroom training of AMT's instruction during a course of aircraft differences; I noted the course does not cover all of the major differences between the aircraft types. This was developed from a longer course to an Overview format; the content has misinformation of nomenclature that Company and [the aircraft manufacturer] do not utilize in their manuals. In depth descriptions of systems and procedures required to maintain aircraft airworthiness is required in the course plan; but it leaves out information and provides insufficient time to completely explain processes and engage the students to answer questions. Time is not available to explain new procedures for IFIM (International Flight Information Manual) use and [other] function is not covered; which is used to assess failures to system components and discrepancies so corrective actions can be taken answering pilot reports to return an aircraft to service. The student book gives pictures with no information as its function is an empty notebook; a new Maintenance Laptop and its Applications are not explained to transfer data to and from the aircraft. This entire program [does not meet the requirements of] FAR Title 14 Chapter I Subchapter G Part 121.375. An AMT with minimal training is 'Maintenance Qualified' but is without complete knowledge of aircraft systems or procedures where airworthiness and safety in a line maintenance environment are important.The revamping of Company training program concentrated on providing minimum instruction that would fully qualifying a new AMT to provide Line Maintenance support immediately. Training is expecting production to have AMT's return to specialized courses; if the AMT would be interested in taking. The [course] leaves the AMT with a trailing edge knowledge base; but they are responsible to FAR's for airworthiness; (as if one could receive a college degree after an indoctrination course and expect to return for years to complete the requirements of the degree) without full knowledge of the aircraft systems. The present development does not build up knowledge to a successful completion of courses; it qualifies one with minimal information and has no requirement to attend any other training thereafter. Developed as a response to limit seat time in a classroom from production to quickly qualify an AMT; this was developed with great objection by seasoned instructors whom left the department or were not part of the development phases. It is not just one aircraft fleet; it incorporates all of Company aircraft maintenance qualifying courses; XXX hr. reduced to XX hr. and XX hr. reduced to XY and X hr. courses; like this Differences course. It will directly affect reliability of departure schedules and airworthiness.Company SMEs must have expert knowledge of the commercial aircraft and the industry and the Company's [training development] department must have extensive aircraft systems knowledge instead of just computer skills for presentations and web based training course development. The training program must ensure that each person who determines the adequacy of work done is fully informed about procedures and techniques and new equipment in use. Remote learning environment cannot substitute an ILT (Instructor-led Training) course. Courses with minimal training information cannot be used in order to qualify AMT's on aircraft fleets. FAA to establish minimum time standards into hours of instruction for qualifying courses and have FAA oversight into content and administration of qualifications. The Maintenance and preventive maintenance training program must ensure competency and completeness (much like Flight Crew Training requirements).
column125: Technician reported that Company training does not meet FAR criteria for qualifications required.

column113: Conflict NMAC
column121: We were beginning to descend from our cruise at 2;500 ft. when Aircraft Y descended on top of us from head on. I hadn't seen the airplane until it filled my windshield. We quickly put Aircraft X into a descending left hand turn because Aircraft Y was slightly to the right side of our aircraft. The plane did not appear to have seen us because they didn't make an effort to turn or climb. It is not uncommon for [university] airplanes to appear completely clueless about whats going on around them. I believe the main cause of the issue was pilot traffic scanning; and maintaining focus outside the cockpit while flying visually. We did not have an ADS-B (Automatic Dependent Surveillance - Broadcast) transponder on board so if they were relying on their ADS-B traffic system to show where we were; then it would not have worked. I don't know why I hadn't found it until as late as it was because I was actively scanning for traffic; and the student I was flying with is a seasoned aviation professional as well. I have already had a conversation with our flight school about staying aware when we are out flying; and will further remind everyone at our next staff meeting.

column121: I'm writing this report because we have guidance in Chapter 15 of the AOM (Aircraft Operating Manual) regarding windshear that is impossible to follow. In a nutshell; we are given black/white guidance in a very gray area. We are told in para 15.5.1; 'Stay clear of thunderstorm cells; heavy precipitation; and areas of known windshear.' Para 15.5.2 says; 'Do not take off if known windshear exists;' and para 15.5.5 says; 'Do not continue the approach if known windshear exists.' But what exactly is 'known' windshear? It is never defined. I have conversed with several of our company experts over the past few months. In all of my discussions with flight standards; flight operations procedures; and various check airmen no one has been able to define 'known' windshear. One subject matter expert told me that an ATC windshear alert; for example; is 'known' windshear; while another told me it was only an advisory. Which is it? All of the company experts I've spoken with admit 'known' windshear is extremely difficult; if not impossible; to define...yet; we're told we must avoid it. AC 00-54 Pilot Windshear Guide; 2.4.2 states; 'The importance of avoiding severe windshear and microbursts cannot be over-emphasized.' Agreed. It goes on to say that 'Unfortunately; there are no universal quantitative windshear avoidance criteria that provide unambiguous go/no-go decision guidelines.' It provides a Table 1 that gives probability of encountering windshear guidelines; but it also admits the information is a 'subjective evaluation of various observational clues to aid in making real time avoidance decisions.'So; there seems to be a wide and varied interpretation within the pilot group; to include our subject matter experts; over what constitutes 'known' windshear. Even the FAA's evaluation criteria for making a go/no-go decision is subjective. It's for these reasons; the AOM should be changed to either define what company means by 'known' windshear; and give examples for clarity; or rewrite the paragraphs cited above to allow for better risk management decision-making. Providing black/white (objective) guidance in a gray (subjective) environment creates confusion; is ambiguous; and can be detrimental to safety when there is not agreement within the pilot group as to what exactly constitutes 'known' windshear.
column125: Reporter describes ambiguous guidance in company manuals with regard to avoiding known windshear.

I was informed that I could be removed from the trip by using my vacation time thereby retaining my pay. The other option was to be removed from the trip without pay and suffer no other adverse consequences. Although I did not believe that the offers to remove myself from the trip were fair; I felt compelled to drop the trip because of my desire to protect myself from potentially being exposed to COVID-19 on the flight deck. The practice of offering crew members the option of taking vacation time is not sustainable. Considering the variables of the extended time COVID-19 may be affecting operations as well as personal choice to refuse mask wearing; one's vacation time could potentially be quickly exhausted. The company mandates pilots that masks be worn in all public places upon reporting to work to include the employee bus; the terminal; and the cabin to protect ourselves and others. This leads me to question why; when COVID-19 cases among pilots are growing; there is no direction strongly encouraging the wearing of masks even when it is not currently FAA directed.

column121: The flight from ZZZ1 to ZZZ was dispatched under our new fuel management program. The 1 hour and 25 minute flight was dispatched with planned fuel of 5830 lbs. The FO and I both noticed and mentioned that the landing fuel numbers were very low for our standard operation. The NAV Log section of the release showed 1;829 lbs. which is planned fuel minus taxi and fuel burn. But the fuel calculation section showed 1;660 lbs. This concerned both the FO and I; as such a long flight with very little fuel to be landing with. We both discussed that the airplane was [under] fueled by at least 200 lbs. Therefore we both felt landing with 2;000 lbs. or worst case scenario 1;800 lbs. was low but a safer and acceptable number. The performance manual shows around 1;700 lbs. at 5;000 ft. is around 45 minutes of fuel. Knowing our fuel was tight; we did what we could to help conserve fuel by delaying the second engine start as long as possible; and we attempted a Flight Level Change climb by several and normal intermediate level off altitudes with only 7 miles in the opposite direction. We continued to monitor our fuel burn. Upon our initial descent; we noticed we had burned more than planned; but we had received a shortcut and still calculated landing with around 1;800 lbs. On a few turns before final; the right fuel gauge began to flash amber at us then continued to flash in and out as fuel moved around throughout the final approach. Upon landing; we had around 1;800 lbs. of fuel. I feel this margin of safety is extremely low for a safety conscious airline and program. The fuel management program should be providing us with more fuel not less fuel. We were [under] fueled and received no undue delays and were proactive in managing and conserving fuel. Our end result was landing with 1;800 lbs. of fuel. In a medium case scenario; if the airport shuts down for an aircraft emergency or anything else; a go around diversion to the closest company destination (ZZZ2 is 78 miles away); say 25 to 30 minutes at 8;000 ft. (1;100 lbs.); would have me landing with 400 lbs. of fuel and I would have had to make that decision immediately. ZZZ3 is the next closest at 117 miles. That is also if those stations both have personnel staffed to service the aircraft. While legal; and probably acceptable; this practice seems to create an unreasonable reduced margin of safety standards with the trade-off being cost savings. In addition; as a Check Airmen and experienced pilot; I have more wherewithal to be neurotically monitoring developing situations; and weather. I can see an average pilot easily being forced into a no-win situation or very unsafe situation with dire outcome.[We] detected and attempted to manage [the issue] upon review of the release and even with proactive measures; the aircraft still landed with an uncomfortable amount of fuel. [We] took proactive fuel burn reductions with no changes in landing fuel. The airline's new fuel management program and a lack of safety margins created by the program [were contributing factors].I suggest changing the standard practice of providing 45 minutes of cruise fuel from flight level altitudes and provide the crew with an accurate and manageable 45 minutes of fuel near 10;000 ft. which is where most people will need that extra fuel in the case of holding; delays; reroutes and diversions which more likely occur below the flight levels. Add fuel and increase the amount of minimum fuel required to be on the airplane for dispatch.

column121: After getting on the aircraft to fly; I did all my pre-flight duties including putting my personal luggage away and doing my safety checks. Just before we were ready to board; the flight crew including myself; was notified that someone had tested positive for COVID-19 from the inbound flight. We were later told that it was a passenger who tested positive for COVID-19. After waiting and many phone calls by the ground management as well as the pilots; it was decided ground personnel would clean the area where the customer had sat more thoroughly than just the tray table areas. The passenger was said to have been sitting in row X that tested positive. The cleaning personnel who wipe down the areas cleaned row X and the surrounding 6 rows behind it as a precaution. They then did it again because the cleaning solution being used says on the directions that it must sit for at least XX minutes on the surfaces being cleaned to properly be effective. Once the cleaning process was finished and completed; we boarded the flight and went on our way.The safety concern here is that there is no protocol for this exact situation and possible exposure. Our whole working crew and whatever ground personnel who entered the plane post flight are possibly exposed to this virus. I personally work in the affected area of the row. I sat in the row waiting for answers as to the protocols on what we were to do next. I touched all overhead bins around row X; the bathroom; bulkhead working station; and all surrounding areas that all could have been affected by this customer who tested positive. It's also not known if this customer had affected any other parts of the cabin and if this virus is on surfaces of the plane in more than just row X and the area of the aircraft that all the crew had to walk on to get on the aircraft.

What I saw as I left the jetway into ZZZ terminal was a little shocking in today's safety and COVID environment.  The end of the terminal had about four flights all trying to load or unload at the same time. The area isn't large but there was at least 1;000 people crammed into the jet way area. No one had room to social distance. Everyone did have a mask on; but it was very uncomfortable. I navigated as well as I could past the crowd as quickly as I could and went much further into the terminal to find an open area. I kept an eye on the loading area from afar but it took a long time to empty the crowd as they boarded. I didn't go back near the gate until about 10 minutes to departure. It was still crowded on my flight and the other boarding planes had crowds around. The jet bridge backed up and our airplane didn't get loaded until just before departure time. I know the agents were trying as best they could; but when that many planes are set to depart at the same time it was difficult. I asked about it and was told that also [another aircraft] had a mechanical and had to de-board all of its passengers into the same crowd as it was awaiting parts.An agent asked our passengers who were at a standstill on the jetway to please get seated quickly; so we didn't have to delay the flight. One passenger said there isn't anywhere to go as the line was just stopped. I feel the management of this boarding process was not well thought out.  If flights are departing that close in time they should be spaced in gates down the concourse apart from each other. If this is the operation each day of the week; it should be looked at to see if there is a better way.

column121: While departing ZZZ Runway X on the RVAV departure; we experienced a wind shear ahead alert shortly after rotation and after the gear was selected up. While climbing through approximately 500 feet AGL; we entered an area of moderate to severe turbulence followed by a gain and loss of 20+ knots of airspeed. Airspeed excisions were brief in nature; and climb rate decreased from near 3;000 FPM to 1;000 FPM and momentarily less while we transited the wind shear. During this time we also experienced multiple quick rolling and yawing moments that required nearly full control deflection to counter.  Both myself and my First Officer agreed that the aircraft was at times momentarily out of control. It is my recollection that the maximum intensity of the turbulence occurred from 500 feet AGL to approximately 3;500 feet AGL. Again these excursions were very quick left and right rolling and yawing motions. During the event; the aircraft energy state never decayed to a dangerous level; however; we were in a lightly loaded -700 with max thrust selected for wind shear avoidance.  Above 4;000 feet; we experienced continuous moderate turbulence and icing up to 14;000 feet; and only light turbulence above that.  There were no injuries reported; and we did not suspect any aircraft damage. The flight attendants and passengers were briefed before takeoff. Before departure; both myself and the First Officer consulted the Airport Wind System reports as well as reviewed the required wind limits for the departure. The ZZZ Tower and Dispatch were contacted to get a better assessment of what they were seeing with the winds as well.  At the time of the beginning of the takeoff roll; the Tower reported steady state wind from the south east 28 KT gusting to 38 KT. The Airport Wind System alert was not functioning; however;  [other weather station's] wind and the gust 2-6 Airport Wind System was reporting. All wind reports were within limits at the time of takeoff; and had been for most of the morning. The Dispatcher reported the 8 High Airport Wind System Alert was intermittent all morning. Both myself and my First Officer briefed the departure and took steps necessary to mitigate the perceived threats. Wind shear take off procedures and power settings were used during the take off.  After level off; myself and the First Officer consulted the FOM and we were in agreement that we experienced a situation where the aircraft was momentarily not in positive control. A pilot report of MOD to SEV turbulence was relayed to ARTCC as well as company. Dispatch was consulted at this time; and a plan was made to divert to ZZZ1 for maintenance inspection as Maintenance did not want the aircraft stuck in ZZZ2. The remainder of the flight was uneventful. Ensuring Airport Wind System has full functionality and wind profiles are working to the greatest extent possible.
column123: Preparing for departure from ZZZ we looked at weather reports including the ZZZ Airport Wind System and PIREPS.  Nothing indicated that we would encounter anything greater than moderate turbulence as we took off from Runway X. Shortly after liftoff we encountered moderate turbulence and at about 1;000 ft AGL received a 'Windshear Ahead' alert.  We then experienced approximately 30 - 45 seconds of severe turbulence. We gave ATC a PIREP and overflew our original destination of ZZZ2 at the request of Dispatch and Maintenance control to get the plane to a maintenance base.
column125: B37-700 flight crew reported after encountering severe turbulence and wind shear; it was decided to overfly the destination airport and divert to a maintenance base.

column121: Following two previous approach attempts into ZZZ1 followed by diversions to our Filed Alternates; on [consecutive evening and morning flights] of Aircraft X. I was advised by Dispatch that if the Crew was willing; they would like us to fly back to ZZZ1 and make yet a third attempt. I immediately said I did not feel safe operating the flight as I didn't feel the approach was safe based on the current conditions at the airport; and the weather we had experienced during the last two attempts. I then stated I would not be willing to make another attempt. During the first attempt (Aircraft X ZZZ-ZZZ1) we experienced moderate turbulence; gusty winds; heavy rain; in IMC at night followed by a Windshear Warning and Escape Maneuver at 150 ft. AGL; following this up with a consecutive approach the next morning (Aircraft X ZZZ2-ZZZ1) resulting in an early Go-Around / Diversion to alternate due to Heavy Rain; Moderate to Severe Turbulence on Approach; Strong Crosswinds on Final; Changing (Increasing) Gusting Wind Conditions on the surface and the previous aircraft making a Severe Turbulence Encounter Report on Final with the Controller inquiring as to passenger injuries on board. This led to a phone call from my direct Supervisor; who tried to convince me the operation was safe. I clearly stated to both persons; that our Dispatch Release reads; and I quote.. 'By Signing this Dispatch Release we are affirming our belief that this flight can be made safely'. I was very clear that after my previous two attempts; I did not feel comfortable operating the flight. The Initial Plan was for us to depart in just 30 minutes after refueling; which again I stated I did not feel safe operating the flight. Followed by yet another phone call from my Supervisor who reviewed the weather with me. Explaining things were much better now and we can depart. Again; I expressed my concerns as to the safety of the flight based on recent experience in the storm system; and inaccuracies experienced with the past weather reports that allowed us to dispatch prior. I felt I was being pushed to operate the flight. My Supervisor continued to tell me of other carriers that made it into ZZZ1 today. Once again I explained they didn't deal with what we had to do; and again relayed I was not comfortable making a flight to ZZZ1 today. After some discussion; it seemed my Supervisor was becoming agitated stating that if I didn't fly he would have to. I remained silent as this was not pertinent to my decision. I was again asked to operate - to which I repeated my opinion that I didn't feel it was a safe operation and I would not even go there as a passenger if you asked me to at that time. I was then told I was being pulled from the flight; I was being relieved of all duty pending an investigation and I would hear from someone in the company. The phone call was then disconnected by the other party.I feel this was an attempt to force a flight crew to accept a flight that I had clearly stated I didn't feel it was safe to operate. I feel that this is a violation of the Captain's joint Authority with Dispatch to operate a flight and is in direct conflict with PIC's (Pilot in Command) responsibility and authority. It also seems to me to be in conflict with the creation of a 'Just Culture: required be FAA in implementing a SMS. A pilot should never be coerced to accept a mission he/she feels is unsafe. In this situation; the PIC was being asked to Dispatch into an area where an URGENT PIREP was broadcast via Center Frequencies advising of Moderate to Severe Turbulence reported below 11;000 ft. by an aircraft on Final Approach to ZZZ1. Another aircraft should not be dispatched to fly an approach in Severe Turbulence. The most basic principle is Dispatchers; Managers; and Supervisors need to listen to the people under their charges. Air Carriers need to stress the importance of Safety; by not just stating they have a culture based on Safety; but by actually exercisingone especially when performance metrics may not be met but safety goals will be achieved. As Pilots; I feel we are all mission-oriented; but the need to complete the mission can never exceed operational safety.
column125: CRJ-200 Pilot reported being pressured to accept flight in unsafe weather conditions.

column119: Company Policy; Environment - Non Weather Related
column121: [I have concerns with] the COVID pre-takeoff PA; which now instructs passenger that flight attendants will collect trash during safety compliance checks. Topics of concern:1. Would this action not be a direct conflict with FAR 121.391 (d)? It is understood that collecting a few used cleaning wipes and some cups does not present an issue. Some may even consider it like collecting pre-departure beverage cups prior to takeoff. However; managing empty pizza boxes (and similar bulky items) will require flight attendants to unlock carts (and/or trash cart doors) since the openings are too narrow to accept such items (FAR 121.577 (c).2. The Flight Attendant could offer to return later for bigger items; but would that not require a judgement call during an extremely limited period? This adds a new distraction from more critical safety matters (seat-belts; tray tables; etc.). A Flight Attendant is responsible for (depending upon aircraft) checking for more than 20 items during routine pre-takeoff/landing compliance checks.3. The Flight Attendant must now carry a trash bag during compliance checks leaving only one hand free to steady themselves from a fall. It is confirmed that taxi surface movement is a high-risk period for injuries. Also; the revised policy now adds more time (additional duty) before being able to secure in a jumpseat.Knowing first-hand the history of Flight Attendant injuries related to surface movement; I strongly recommend the company reevaluate and consider changing to a more reasonable expectation of trash collection after takeoff.
column125: Flight Attendant reported concerns regarding the COVID-19 pre-takeoff PA instructing passengers to give the FAs trash during the safety compliance check. Flight Attendant believes this detracts from the main reason for the safety check.

column113: Deviation / Discrepancy - Procedural Published Material / Policy; Deviation / Discrepancy - Procedural FAR; Deviation / Discrepancy - Procedural Maintenance
column121: Arrived at the airport at XA:45. The station representative met us advising he had just received an email further pushing our departure back to XE:00. He also advised they were unsure as to whether there were even parts on hand to repair the aircraft correctly and wondered if it would even depart that day. We settled in the crew rest area to wait and see. At XC:51; I received a call from a Company A representative asking how we felt and if we were still good to fly. They said Maintenance advised they would have the plane fixed in about an hour. We advised we were good to go. I then called Dispatch to advise and keep them in the loop. The Dispatcher emailed us release revision 2. We reviewed it and headed out to the aircraft. As we arrived outside the aircraft; Maintenance was still aboard working on the aircraft. At XD:35; Maintenance finished with the aircraft. We then boarded the aircraft to find it on battery power and the GPU (Ground Power Unit) running but the over voltage warning light indicator on. We flagged down personnel to get a new GPU. At XD:45 the APU (Auxiliary Power Unit) was started. The replacement GPU arrived as the APU completed powering up. We then began our preflight. At XE:20; the preflight was finished. At XE:35; Maintenance finished the logbook and maintenance brief. We then blocked out. During this time I became aware of Company A Maintenance Control pressuring the on-site Company B maintenance personnel to 'line out' the other maintenance personnel's write-up stating 'Undocumented speed tape found concealing stripped and unsecured mounting screw to extruded MCD (Main Cabin Door) Manual Vent Door Drive (Pressure Vessel) cover panel'. This was found in Aircraft X's logbook. Maintenance Control wanted this done so as not to draw scrutiny to it by the FAA. Thankfully and appreciatively the Company B Mechanic refused to assist in this cover-up.This is the consistent behavior I have observed from the pressure Company C exerts on this operation to dispatch the aircraft on time at all costs. In the past several years I have submitted safety reports and FCRs (Flight Crew Reports) observing willful and wanton disregard of DDG (Dispatch Deviation Guide) procedures plainly written out. I've listened to Maintenance Control; on speakerphone; tell an on-site mechanic to 'Write whatever the hell makes the pilot happy to get that damn thing out of there.' I've had Maintenance Control denigrate me for not immediately pulling back into the gate with a problem but instead I followed procedure and called Dispatch for a three-way call with the problem. I've observed on numerous occasions and numerous stations Company B mechanics back-dooring the fuel sheets to make the numbers work instead of obtaining the fuel density to compute the numbers correctly to check the accuracy of the fueling process. I've had Company B mechanics on my jump seat share experiences of Maintenance Control telling them to check good" a system without actually testing the system. Thankfully as well; those mechanics told Maintenance Control their tickets were more important to them than their jobs. Both of those mechanics have since left Company B for more ethical workplace environments.My observation is the attitude in Maintenance Control is a mixture of arrogance and fear-driven to kowtow to this pressure. The pressure is passed down the chain from Company C to the executive level to the middle managers to the line supervisors to the line workers. This culture is a very dangerous one. It severely erodes the necessary safety and trust in the process required for it to work as it is designed. I have personally had so many of these instances where I have found incorrectly complied with DDG procedures both by blatant disregard and ignorance. I and several of my colleagues have experienced an extreme increase in time and concern when reviewing the log and maintenance procedures. I personally am not trained as a mechanic. The amount of items that we can see and have found greatly concerns us. Our concern is the amount of Maintenance egregious violations and errors we can see are so numerous and regular; how safe; complete; and compliant is the work; like the heavy checks and line maintenance behind the panels and cowlings that we cannot see. Our fear is this is going to lead to injuries; loss of life; and more aircraft damage and hull losses. The solution is straight forward and already established. Enforce strict adherence to maintaining the aircraft and equipment as directed by our FAA-approved maintenance program."
column125: Air Carrier Captain reported non-adherence to proper aircraft maintenance procedures.

column121: Many of our fellow pilots have said to me that many pilots and others have been affected by COVID in one way or another; either necessitating quarantine or even hospitalization. Further; and in particularly our fleet; there is a disruption to the training schedules due to a lack of instructors; hence little; if any continuity. Have the Union and the Company considered shutting down for another month as the surge upon surge rages? There's no need to rush training if pilots will go non-current without the additional flying. Plus an additional month might get us to the beginning of a vaccination.I don't know if I have missed communications or not from the Union or the Company; but what is the plan for vaccinating pilots or other front line employees? Is there a timetable? Where do we stand in the national 'Phase 1' rollout? Has an update to the memorandum on pay during COVID sick leave been considered? Specifically; what happens to the pilot who receives round 1 or 2 of the vaccination and has to call in sick due to side effects. What happens to a pilot who has adverse side effects which lead to LTD? Could this pilot apply for the new LTD benefits and not wait until month X? Is a pilot required to take the vaccination? If yes; what happens if he declines? Where will pilots get the vaccination? Does a pilot have to wait until the Company procures the vaccine? What has FAA medical said about any of this?

column121: We were fully boarded; with checklist Before Start To The Line complete. Flight Attendant B reported passenger submitted; when Flight Attendant A reported the ramp agents requested we contact Station Ops.  Operations informed us Flight Attendant B was being replaced on the trip by a reserve. I assisted Flight Attendant B off loading bags; and advised them to catch a breath and gather all effects. The pressure of the last minute situation and the ramp; and the timeline; needed to be ignored at that moment. The replacement flight attendant arrived; I assisted loading bags; verified ID and returned to the flight deck. Weight and performance numbers came up and the Captain stated ready for door closure. The main cabin door and cockpit door secured; weights entered and verified on FMS. Without communication from ramp agents; they re-opened the main cabin door. Flight Attendant A knocked on the cockpit door; which we opened. Flight Attendant A stated the First Officer was now also being replaced on the trip. I queried Crew Scheduling and was told name ordered me off the trip with no further information given. The replacement First Officer arrived and I offloaded the bags. The Captain was astonished that such an action would take place without communication; consult; or apparent consideration for safety of flight.Passengers expressed surprise; and some measure of concern and alarm. The main cabin door being opened without the crew's awareness is not a procedure I have seen before. Replacing crew members who are not ill at the last minute affects our morale and pocketbook; and creates unnecessary concern for the revenue passengers. Safety of flight is inevitably affected by this kind of turmoil; and it falls on the Company to alleviate stress and safety rather than contribute to it. The Company exercised authority in contradiction to common sense and safety of flight. The timeline; already compressed; became distressingly so given the apparent chaos this action caused in view of passengers. Ordering the ramp to open the main cabin door without communicating first to the crew is an extraordinary act of harassment and intimidation. If a pilot needs to be replaced on a trip for administrative reasons; surely that can wait until after a short turn is completed. This action requires the crew; the new crew; to re-brief and reinitialize a flight under stress; delays our passengers; compromises our timeline; and potentially costs us our bonuses. It should be taken only under extraordinary circumstances; which apparently do not exist. The Company should be an asset to safety of flight rather than a liability.
column125: An Air Carrier First Officer reported ramp agents opened the main cabin door without coordinating with the crew and ordered the First Officer and B Flight Attendant off the aircraft.

column121: On Day 5; the crew desk called me to assign me to a trip on Day 6 with a XA:40 report time.  I called in fatigued on Day 6 at approximately [3 hours prior]. I had just finished a 4 day trip from Day 2 to Day 5; with each day requiring subsequently later report times and the last 2 days requiring late or all night flying.  Additionally; even though this trip spanned 4 calendar days; it only included 2 sleep cycles.The previous 4 day trip created a significant cumulative sleep debt; as tracked my my Apple Watch. Day 1-Day 2; 6:38 of sleep at home prior to reporting for my 4 day trip. Day 2-Day 3; 8:44 of sleep in the ZZZ hotel. Day 3-Day 4; 5:26 of sleep after landing in ZZZ1; but I woke up at XA:00 am local time and had a XR:59 departure that night.  I attempted to take a nap prior to reporting for duty but I couldn't fall asleep and was awake for about 18 hours prior to flying a redeye. Day 3-Day 4; No sleep due to redeye.  I flew a redeye.  Extremely tired during the flight and took a 25 minute nap during cruise.  When I returned home; I slept from approximately XA:45 until the crew desk woke me from deep sleep shortly after XG:00.  After the crew desk woke me; I packed my bags and prepared for the new trip.  I attempted to go back to bed at XK:00 but could not fall asleep.  I realized that it was not safe for me to attempt to fly my 3rd transcontinental trip in 2 days so I called in fatigued. Day 5-Day 6; No sleep due to crew desk interrupting my recovery sleep from the previous redeye.Of note; my previous trip involved a significant cumulative clockwise circadian rhythm disruption in which every day required a later report time and each day flew later into the night.  Clockwise disruptions such as this are generally easier to tolerate compared to counter-clockwise disruptions.  However; this 4 day trip also was engineered to accumulate sleep debt.  Day 1 was 11 hours of duty and 20.5 hours of rest; which equates to a 31.5 hour long duty/sleep cycle.  Day 2 of my 4 day was 7:15 of duty and nearly 25 hours of rest; which was a 32 hour duty/sleep cycle.  Combined with each day's later duty periods; it was generally easy to fall asleep at the beginning of the rest periods.  However; it was not realistic nor possible to achieve 2 circadian rests or resets during the layovers; which meant that each day I would be awake for a longer period of time prior to reporting for late night flights.  This culminated when I reported for my redeye after being awake for 18 hours.  Upon landing I was absolutely exhausted and needed to catch up on the previous 4 days of missed sleep.  Unfortunately the crew desk interrupted this much-needed sleep and assigned me the new trip on Day 6.  In other words; the crew desk expected me to shift from my previous XM:00 pm and XS:00 pm local report times to a XA:40 am report time after disrupting my rest and being awake for over 12 hours.  Furthermore; the crew desk did not consider the larger picture of the 4 day trip and subsequent 2 day trip as a single 6 day long physiological marathon.  Considering the operational design of my previous 4 day trip; combined with a back-to-back 2 day trip; I do not think it's physiological possible or safe for anyone to endure such a disruptive 6 day period especially after the crew desk wakes you up in the middle of a much needed restorative sleep period.

column121: I am writing this because I'm concerned about the safety of the ZZZ - ZZZZ - Chengdu Airport - ZZZZ - ZZZ pairing.  Starting on date; it was changed from a 4 person crew to a 3 person crew. This is NOT SAFE. I understand why [it] changed is because the duty time went from XX:25 to XX:20 and so only 3 pilots are required.  I've flown this trip X times and it's challenging with 4 pilots let alone 3 pilots.  The primary purpose of this report is to identify this [pairing] as a safety event. My goal is to provide a corrective measure that will mitigate a safety issue; before it becomes a Incident/AccidentTHREAT: Personal Fatigue.  This trip is on the back side of the body clock with a late night pick up out of ZZZZ. This trip becomes close to a XX hour duty day because of the 1:10 ride from downtown [city] to the airport and back.  The flight from ZZZZ is often 2 hours longer than the flight coming back from Chengdu due to the Jet stream.  Often; because of this jet stream; there is moderate turbulence along the route; making sleeping in the bunk difficult.  The Captain must be in his seat for the take-off and landings for both legs.  With a three pilot crew; it is difficult coming up with a plan for breaks; especially on the shorter Chengdu - ZZZZ segment. The relief Pilot will hardly get a break; besides being upset for not getting a break; they will be very tired. The relief pilot is one of the most important people in the crew; and you want them alert. They often see threats before the flying pilots do; and can bring the threat to their attention.THREAT: Environmental.  Often there is low visibility in Chengdu and ZZZZ this time of year; increasing the work load. There is a X:10 break in Chengdu which is a very busy time for the pilots. We have to flight plan; pre-flight (with full suit) deal with the Chengdu police and customs. With the boarding of cargo (very noisy and moves the plane around a lot with loading of cargo containers) it is impossible to get any rest while on the ground.THREAT: Technical.  Chinese airlines are almost back to normal; leaving Chengdu is a very busy time for ATC. I have flown with new pilots that haven't flown China and between operating in meters and the difficulty with the Chinese ATC language it is impossible; and irresponsible leaving the flight deck out of FL180 to go on break. Because of this; it isn't until reaching cruise altitude until the Captain can start his break.  Meters can be challenging and causes a lot of missed ATC calls; or repeats. The 4th pilot is a huge benefit for listening to ATC and providing back up!I've flown this trip X times now; having 2 Captains and 2 FO's was by far the safest. It gave everyone a break and each Captain had a leg which helped mitigate stress; and fatigue.  With one Captain and 3 FO's it is not nearly as safe as 2 Captains; but it is bearable because you get more of a break. With one Captain and 2 FO's it is impossible to get a break; of good rest.Another thing the company needs to think about; this is China during COVID times.  The company realizes the difficulty of a layover in China for the crews. The duty time with 3 crews is reduced to XX hours. There can only be a X:10 delay in Chengdu before an extension takes place. After doing this trip X times I know for a fact that the likelihood of me extending is nil; due to fatigue.The mitigation is simple. Make it a 4 person crew.

column121: On [Date] I was given the work assignment of running the #1 engine on Aircraft X for a BUG (Back-Up Generator) operational and leak check by my lead mechanic; ref log page. I knew that there was a fuel leak in the pylon for the #1 engine as I had written it up the day before; ref log page. At this time the left wing had 30;000 lbs of fuel in it to help find the leak source into the pylon. I declined to run the engine to verify the operation of the relatively minor repair of the BUG replacement compared to the danger of fire from an active fuel leak into the #1 engine's pylon. I explained my reasoning to the pair of shift foreman. They went away and returned with the directive to 'Run the Engine' regardless of the fuel leak or my concerns. The foreman said the Operating Manager had directed this.  At this point I decided to run the engine as they ordered me to do rather than risk losing my job. I ran the engine with an eye for protecting my work partners and the aircraft as much as possible given the situation management forced me into. As the days have gone by since this happened it has bothered me more and more until I felt I had to reach out to the [Aviation Safety Action Program] and Ground Safety committees. Please take note of [Company] Engine Run Policy [Number].'At no time will safety be compromised for operational reasons'.

column121: After nearly a 12 hour duty day on date and over 8 hrs of actual flight time my crew and I were advised on the way to our layover hotel that one of our crew members tested positive for COVID.  Once we arrived at the Hotel the crew member that was positive was sent to another hotel [to] quarantine.  The rest of us were told we had to be back downstairs to return to base for a XE:45am pickup.  It was nearly XA:00am by this time.  I told the individual that called us we were not legal and needed our rest.  The idea of being exposed to COVID and now no opportunity to sleep was unacceptable.  He stated that we had to be on the first flight available to return to base.  We were not going to continue to argue; so we did as we were told.  He said this was just a deadhead but that wasn't the point; we needed our rest and there was another flight several hours later that potentially we might have been legal for.  We were on duty for more than 20 hours with no additional pay or compensation.  COVID or not our contract should have been honored under these circumstances most especially.  Does COVID negate FAR's and Contractual agreements?

column121: I was cleared on the RNAV departure to maintain 3;000 feet. When loading the departure an additional fix not noted on the chart was loaded in. I reloaded the departure to ensure I had the proper transition and the fix appeared again. This fix is not on the RNAV departure. I later realized that this is how the G1000 creates additional heading legs; but at the time I did not realize this and so I deleted the extraneous fix to have the flight plan reflect the proper departure.  After reaching 1;000 feet the departure states to turn left heading 295; which I had briefed. I began turning right and was adjusting the heading bug and noted it was taking a long time to reach 295 before I realized that I was turning the wrong direction. I corrected and initiated the turn back to the left heading 295. I was about 20 to 30 degrees off from the heading when ATC called and asked if I was VFR or IFR. I responded with that I was IFR and turning left to 295.There are a few contributing factors that lead to my error. First was my general lack of recency with the G1000. In the last 120 days I had amassed a total of only 6.7 hours in the aircraft. Additionally I only have a total of 24 hours in the aircraft since my check-ride last summer. This lack of familiarity; currency and comfort in the aircraft contributed to the error.  After passing 1;000 feet the G1000 was showing a magenta line to the right which is probably partially why I turned the wrong direction. An additional contributing factor was that I was operating single pilot IFR in icing conditions with little experience in the aircraft. This was a Part 91 flight and the 100 hour rule for commuter passenger operations did not apply.   While the General Operations Manual has a dual qualification policy it does not adequately address low time pilots. When a pilot is only qualified in one aircraft type they are afforded an opportunity immediately after completing their check-ride and Operational Experience to gain significant flight time and consolidate their knowledge and skills in the aircraft over the course of a few months.  In my scenario I have accumulated only 9 hours since the completion of Operational Experience. I am in full compliance with the policy and have been active in ensuring I fly the aircraft within the 90 days and make an effort to coordinate a flight when I haven't flown in over 30 days. As is evidenced by my low hours recently this does not afford a pilot new in the aircraft any significant flight time; nor the ability to consolidate their knowledge. While all aircraft are of the general purpose multi-engine variety this aircraft is the only Technically Advanced Aircraft (TAA) in the fleet. This adds a significant amount of complexity to it's operation.I would suggest that when a pilot is qualified on any aircraft we afford them the necessary time and hours to consolidate their knowledge and skills. Part 121 recognizes this issue and has the requirement for consolidation of knowledge and skills in new type aircraft. While again our aircraft are of the same type I feel with dramatic difference in this aircraft particularly; being a TAA; there should be a similar effort. Under Part 121 a pilot would be required to obtain 100 hours within 120 days of becoming qualified in the aircraft. I think we should model a policy to have similar protections. Consolidation was identified in a joint government/industry task force in 1993 to be of concern. One primary reason was 'in recent years manufacturers have introduced a greater number of new aircraft containing more equipment and systems variations within type. Pilots must not only learn different aircraft handling characteristics but they must also be able to work with a variety of aircraft equipment such as automated flight control and flight management systems.' (Federal Register Vol 60 No 81). As this aircraft aligns closely with the concerns listed here that resulted in the regulatory change (advanced aircraft systems)I think a policy to ensure a similar amount of experience is obtained in each aircraft a pilot is qualified in. This would not be to change the dual qualification policy; however; it would most often be triggered and impact pilots who are dual qualified. If a pilot were unable to accomplish the consolidation of knowledge and skills then I think it would be best to enter them into a recurrent training program. As a direct result of this deviation and review of my recent experience I have requested a training event from the flight training department to ensure that I have the requisite proficiency and after that I will make a concerted effort to add shifts to ensure I can achieve my consolidation of knowledge and skills.

column121: Upon closing the main cabin door to the aircraft; the Captain (other person) broke the loop end of the door handle from the main cabin door off. The door was still functional as it could be opened and closed; however as the part was broken; I argued that we were AOG (Aircraft On Ground) until it got fixed. The part is not in the MEL (Minimum Equipment List) / CDL (Configuration Deviation List). The Captain disagreed and stated that her conversation with the company said we were good to go. I had reservations about the broken part and made them known to both her and the company however the initial repositioning leg (empty) and two charter (Part 135) legs thereafter with passengers were flown. Only in a conversation today did I realize that the Captain did not actually report the problem in its entirety to our maintenance department for a full evaluation of the issue.The plane was fixed the following day.I have had a conversation with the company and may likely be resigning this position as this incident is indicative of a much larger; systemic problem within this company regarding maintenance culture. Operations will likely be making changes to the staff based on this event but I am not confident in this pilot position causing a future accident / incident / violation. I had been chastised about not following the chain of command in reporting maintenance issues in the past which is why I did not speak up further during this incident. I personally will never again put myself in a situation where an obvious maintenance issue such as this overrides common sense (and likely a few [CFR]'s) (Code of Federal Regulations).
